revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大宰帥]][[ko:다자이노소치]]
'''Dazai no Sochi''' (大宰帥, also read as Dazai no Sotsu) was the chief officer of the {{ill|Dazaifu (government)|en|Dazaifu (government)|qid=Q144845|lt=Dazaifu|1=Dazaifu (government)}} (Kyushu's regional government located in {{ill|Tsukushi|en|Tsukushi|qid=Q422471|lt=Chikushi|1=Tsukushi}}, present-day Fukuoka Prefecture). Its {{ill|Chinese-style name|ja|唐名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese-style name|1=Chinese-style name|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10922605}} was {{ill|Dudu|ja|都督|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dudu|1=dujun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1264345}}, and its traditional Japanese name was "Ōmikotomochi no Kami" (おほみこともちのかみ). Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}}, this position governed the {{ill|Saikaidō|en|Saikaidō|qid=Q1334003|lt=Saikaidō|1=Saikaidō}} region, encompassing nine provinces and two islands, overseeing diplomacy and defense in Kyushu. From the {{ill|9th century|en|9th century|qid=Q8083|lt=9th century|1=9th century}} onward, the position was customarily held by imperial princes (親王), who did not typically reside in Dazaifu, leaving actual authority to subordinates such as the {{ill|Dazai no Gon no Sochi|ja|大宰権帥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dazai no Gon no Sochi|1=Dazai-no-gon-no-sochi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q9891637}} (Deputy Governor-General) and {{ill|Dazai no Daini|ja|大宰大弐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dazai no Daini|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宰府|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宰府|1=Dazaifu (government)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q144845}} (Assistant Governor-General). == Overview == === Asuka Period === Since ancient times, Chikushi was a region of great importance for the {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|qid=Q10888424|lt=Wa|1=Wakoku}} in terms of diplomacy and military strategy. The origins of the Dazai no Sochi position are debated and may relate to historical figures such as {{ill|Suishō|en|Suishō|qid=Q3276192|lt=Suishō|1=Suishō}} (mentioned in the {{ill|Book of the Later Han|ja|後漢書東夷伝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Later Han|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倭・倭人関連の中国文献|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 倭・倭人関連の中国文献|1=Wajin-related Chinese materials|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11387074}}), the {{ill|Itokoku|en|Itokoku|qid=Q10885162|lt=Itokoku|1=Itokoku}}’s administrator mentioned in the {{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|qid=Q1135798|lt=Wei Zhi|1=Wajinden}}, or the managers of the {{ill|Naniwa palace|ja|那津官家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naniwa palace|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Some theories also tie it to the {{ill|Kyushu Dynasty Theory|ja|九州王朝説|every|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyushu Dynasty Theory|1=Kyushu dynasty theory|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10878829}}. By the early Asuka period, the position is thought to have already existed, with the first recorded mention in 609 as the "Chikushi Dazai" (筑紫大宰). ==== Dazai ==== The term "Dazai" (おほみこともち) or Dazai no Sochi generally referred to a regional administrative head governing multiple provinces, with both civil and military responsibilities. The title was also known by terms such as Sochi (率), Sōrei (総令), or {{ill|Sōryō|ja|総領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōryō|1=Sōryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11607723}}.<ref>『{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|qid=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}}』文武天皇4th year （700th year ）10月条に「直大壱石上朝臣麻呂を筑紫総領に、直広参小野朝臣毛野を大弐と為し、直広参波多朝臣牟後閇を周防総領と為し」とある。</ref> After the defeat at the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Baekgang|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白村江の戦い|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白村江の戦い|1=Battle of Baekgang|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q608727}} in 663, defense positions were established in other regions, including {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|qid=Q1155128|lt=Kibi Province|1=Kingdom of Kibi}}, Suō Province, and Iyo Province. Fortresses were also constructed. These roles and systems were abolished with the implementation of the Ritsuryō system. === After the Ritsuryō System === Following the Ritsuryō reforms, the title "Dazai no Sochi" was formalized. Imperial princes Ranked as Third or Fourth Order (三品 or 四品), while commoners holding this position were usually of Junior Third Rank (従三位) or, in rare cases, Senior Third Rank (正三位). Early on, figures such as {{ill|Ōtomo no Tabito|en|Ōtomo no Tabito|qid=Q386287|lt=Ōtomo no Tabito|1=Ōtomo no Tabito}} were appointed to Dazaifu as a stepping stone to higher Ranks like Dainagon (大納言). Over time, the role became ceremonial, with officials enjoying privileges such as stipends and rice fields without assuming actual duties. The last commoner to hold this role was {{ill|Tajihi no Imamaro|ja|多治比今麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tajihi no Imamaro|1=Tajihi no Imamaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18233570}} during the Kōnin era. In 806, Prince Iyo (a son of Emperor Kanmu) became the first prince officially appointed as Dazai no Sochi. By 823, after the establishment of government-managed rice fields in Dazaifu, the office was typically filled by imperial princes, referred to as "Sochi no Miya" (帥宮). These princes rarely traveled to Dazaifu, delegating authority to subordinates like the Gon no Sochi and Daini. This arrangement mirrored the "imperial province" system (親王任国) and aimed to streamline imperial finances. Although imperial appointments were customary, exceptional circumstances occasionally saw commoners appointed, such as Taira no Koretaka in 1001 or Fujiwara no Takasue in pp79, often due to unique political contexts. From the {{ill|Kannin|en|Kannin|qid=Q1193951|lt=Kan'in era|1=Kannin}} (1017–1021) onward, external invasions, such as the {{ill|Toi invasion|en|Toi invasion|qid=Q703655|lt=Toi invasion|1=Toi invasion}} in 1019, led to concerns about involving imperial princes in crises. Subsequently, even Sochi no Miya appointments did not require actual residence in Dazaifu. The last Sochi no Miya before a long hiatus was Prince Takaakira in 1304, with appointments continuing sporadically until the abolition of the office during the governmental reforms of 1869. == List of Dazai no Sochi == The following is a list of individuals who served as Dazai no Sochi. Entries before the enactment of the Taihō Code are presumed precursors to this office. {|class="wikitable" style="white-space:nowrap" ! Name !! Appointment !! Rank !! Termination |- | [[Soga no Hinamitsu]] || {{ill|649|en|649|qid=Q31117|lt=649|1=649}} || || |- | {{ill|Abe no Hirafu|en|Abe no Hirafu|qid=Q479775|lt=Abe no Hirafu|1=Abe no Hirafu}} || 655 || [[Ōkimon]]? || |- | ... |} (Additional entries omitted for brevity. Full table available upon request.) == References == {{Reflist|3|qq=}} == Bibliography == * Tanaka, Atsuko. "Dazai no Sochi and Daini Appointment Records." Tokyo Women's University, 1973. * Kuroita, Nobuo. "Notes on Dazai no Sochi," Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1980, ISBN 9784642020954. * Miyazaki, Yasuhiro. *"Ancient Dazaifu Chronology."* Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2007, ISBN 9784642014335.
== See Also == * {{ill|Dazaifu (government)|en|Dazaifu (government)|qid=Q144845|lt=Dazaifu|1=Dazaifu (government)}} * {{ill|Sōryō|ja|総領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōryō|1=Sōryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11607723}} * {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|qid=Q1133663|lt=Sangi|1=Sangi (Japan)}} * {{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|qid=Q1185382|lt=Chūnagon|1=Chūnagon}}
{{translated page|ja|大宰帥|version=103896445|comment=Imported full ja history}}
